past_medical_history: Neurofibromatosis type 1, diagnosed in childhood based on cafe-au-lait spots and multiple cutaneous neurofibromas
Left optic pathway glioma identified at age 9, managed conservatively with serial imaging, stable for over 20 years
Excision of subcutaneous neurofibroma from left forearm at age 22
Iron-deficiency anemia, resolved after oral supplementation 2 years ago
Migraine without aura, intermittent since adolescence
family_history: Father has neurofibromatosis type 1 with multiple cutaneous neurofibromas and a history of plexiform neurofibroma of the right thigh
Paternal grandmother died of unknown brain tumor at age 55
One younger brother, unaffected by NF1

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, approximately once a month
social_history.occupation: Graphic designer, works primarily at a computer
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in a second-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Regular yoga and cycling until 2 weeks ago when leg weakness made cycling difficult

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without word-finding difficulty. Comprehension intact for complex commands. Attention intact — performs serial sevens without error. Memory 3/3 immediate recall and 3/3 at five minutes. No evidence of cognitive impairment. Affect is appropriate but patient appears anxious about her symptoms.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light and accommodation. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No ptosis. Facial sensation intact bilaterally in V1, V2, and V3 distributions. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Gag reflex intact. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: deltoid 4/5, biceps 4+/5, triceps 4/5, wrist extensors 4/5, grip 4/5, finger abductors 4-/5. Left upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: hip flexion 4/5, knee extension 4+/5, ankle dorsiflexion 4/5, great toe extension 4/5. Left lower extremity: hip flexion 4+/5, knee extension 4+/5, ankle dorsiflexion 4+/5, great toe extension 4+/5. Mild pronator drift on the right. No fasciculations or significant atrophy. Increased tone in bilateral lower extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reduced light touch and pinprick sensation in a band-like distribution across the upper chest bilaterally (approximately C5-T2 level). Proprioception mildly diminished at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense reduced at ankles bilaterally. Temperature sensation diminished in right upper extremity. No sensory level definitively identified on exam. Upper extremities show patchy hypoesthesia more prominent on the right.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 3+ bilaterally. Triceps 3+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 3+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally with bilateral crossed adductors. Achilles 3+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are extensor bilaterally (bilateral Babinski sign). Sustained ankle clonus (5-6 beats) bilaterally. Hoffmann sign positive bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric on the right with intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing impaired bilaterally with irregular trajectory. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed in the right hand.
neurological_exam.gait: Spastic gait with circumduction more prominent on the right. Narrow-based but stiff. Unable to tandem walk. Romberg positive with sway in all directions.
neurological_exam.additional: Multiple cafe-au-lait macules greater than 1.5 cm visible on trunk and extremities (more than 6 identified). Several small cutaneous neurofibromas palpable on the trunk and upper arms. Bilateral palpable subcutaneous nodules in the occipital region consistent with neurofibromas. Lhermitte sign positive on neck flexion. No papilledema on fundoscopic examination.

patient.chief_complaint: Four days of progressive right arm weakness and sensory changes in both legs
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Lucia Navarro is a 34-year-old right-handed woman with a longstanding history of neurofibromatosis type 1 who presents to the emergency department with a four-day history of rapidly progressive motor weakness involving her right arm and sensory disturbances affecting both lower extremities. She reports that approximately four days ago, she noticed difficulty gripping her coffee cup with her right hand while preparing for work. Over the following 24 hours, the weakness progressed to involve the entire right upper extremity, making it difficult for her to use her computer mouse or hold objects securely. Concurrently, she developed an uncomfortable tingling and numbness sensation in both feet that ascended to involve her calves and thighs over the next two days. She describes a tight, band-like squeezing sensation around her mid-chest that worsens with deep breathing, which she initially attributed to anxiety. Two days ago, she noticed increasing stiffness and heaviness in both legs when climbing the stairs to her apartment, and her partner observed that her gait had become noticeably stiff and unsteady. She has also experienced a new electric shock-like sensation radiating down her spine and into her legs when she bends her neck forward, which she finds alarming. She denies any preceding fever, recent infections, vaccinations, or systemic symptoms. There has been no bowel or bladder dysfunction, although she notes some mild urinary urgency that she had attributed to her increased fluid intake. She has had no headache, visual changes, facial weakness, or difficulty swallowing. She denies any recent trauma or unusual physical activity. Her migraines have been at their usual baseline frequency. She reports that her NF1-related symptoms, including her cutaneous neurofibromas, have been stable for years, and her childhood optic glioma has not required treatment. She is particularly worried because her father, who also has NF1, developed a large plexiform neurofibroma that required surgical intervention, and her paternal grandmother died of a brain tumor. She has not had a neurological assessment or spinal imaging in over three years.

background.reactivity: Normal reactivity to eye opening and closure
background.sleep_architecture: Not assessed during this 30-minute routine recording

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response bilaterally without photoparoxysmal response

The findings are nonspecific and may reflect subcortical or brainstem pathology, medication effects, or metabolic derangement. Clinical correlation is recommended.

Repeat EEG if clinical status changes

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord, extending from C2 level through C7, with cranial extension to the lower medulla oblongata including the area postrema
findings.size: Longitudinally extensive, spanning approximately 12 cm in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense to isointense relative to normal cord
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous hyperintensity throughout the involved segments with areas of central signal void suggesting hemorrhage or necrosis
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal extending throughout the lesion with ill-defined margins
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Heterogeneous peripheral and eccentric enhancement pattern involving both intramedullary and exophytic components; homogeneous peripheral enhancement of the main mass

findings.mass_effect: Marked expansion of the cervical spinal cord with near-complete effacement of the surrounding CSF space. The exophytic component extends anterolaterally, displacing but not clearly invading adjacent structures.
findings.borders: Ill-defined margins with infiltrative appearance; the cranial extent blends with the lower medulla

findings.location: Arising from the cervical cord and extending anterolaterally into the intradural extramedullary space
findings.size: Approximately 2.5 x 1.8 cm in maximum axial dimension
findings.borders: Relatively well-defined smooth border with the CSF space
Well-defined but lobulated, following the course of the facial nerve

additional_observations: No intracranial parenchymal mass lesion identified on brain MRI sequences
Ventricles are normal in size and configuration
No abnormal intracranial enhancement aside from the epicranial lesions
Mild cord edema extending caudally to T1-T2 level beyond the enhancing lesion margins
No evidence of leptomeningeal enhancement or drop metastases in the imaged portions of the thoracic spine
mri.impression: Extensive intraspinal intradural infiltrative expansile lesion involving nearly the entire cervical spinal cord from C2 through C7 with cranial extension to the lower medulla oblongata including the area postrema region. The lesion demonstrates heterogeneous peripheral and eccentric enhancement with both intramedullary and exophytic components. In the context of NF1, the most common intramedullary tumor would be pilocytic astrocytoma, but the imaging characteristics here — including the heterogeneous enhancement pattern, areas suggesting central necrosis, and the extensive infiltrative nature — are atypical for pilocytic astrocytoma and raise concern for a higher-grade glial neoplasm. The exophytic component and involvement of the area postrema region could also suggest consideration of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, though the enhancement pattern is more suggestive of neoplasm. Bilateral occipital subcutaneous lesions consistent with neurofibromas. Left parotid lesion with imaging features suggestive of plexiform neurofibroma.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Most likely given heterogeneous enhancement, central necrosis, and infiltrative pattern
Considered given NF1 association but imaging features are atypical (lacks classic cystic mass with mural nodule pattern)
Possible differential for intramedullary spinal cord tumor; has been reported in association with NF1
Lower likelihood; area postrema involvement raises consideration but enhancement pattern favors neoplasm

recommended_actions: Urgent neurosurgical consultation for tissue diagnosis
MR spectroscopy of the lesion to further characterize metabolite profile
Complete spinal axis MRI to evaluate for drop metastases
Consider MRI perfusion if technically feasible for spinal cord lesion
Serological testing for AQP4 antibodies and MOG antibodies to exclude demyelinating etiologies

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with no acute abnormalities. Normal intervals and axis.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardiac abnormalities identified. Baseline ECG is unremarkable.

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated TSH; may represent subclinical hypothyroidism or effect of oral contraceptive on thyroid-binding globulin. Could contribute to fatigue but does not explain the acute neurological presentation.

Other.clinical_significance: Mildly low despite supplementation; unlikely to be clinically significant in the acute setting

labs.interpretation: Largely unremarkable laboratory studies. Mildly elevated TSH with normal free T4 suggests possible subclinical hypothyroidism, which may warrant follow-up but does not explain the acute myelopathic presentation. Low-normal vitamin D. CBC, BMP, liver function, coagulation, and inflammatory markers are within normal limits. No evidence of systemic infection or inflammation. Normal B12 and folate exclude nutritional myelopathy.

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord lesion at C4-C5 level (enhancing component)

signal_characteristics.choline: Markedly elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio 3.8, normal <2.0)
signal_characteristics.NAA: Markedly reduced N-acetylaspartate peak (NAA/Cr ratio 0.6, normal >1.5)
signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid-lactate peak present, consistent with necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

additional_observations: Spectroscopy pattern is consistent with a high-grade glial neoplasm
The elevated choline reflects high cellular turnover and membrane synthesis
Reduced NAA indicates neuronal loss or displacement
Lipid-lactate peak suggests areas of necrosis, characteristic of high-grade tumors
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates a metabolic profile strongly suggestive of a high-grade glial neoplasm with elevated choline, reduced NAA, and a lipid-lactate peak indicating necrosis. These findings, combined with the imaging characteristics, are most consistent with glioblastoma or anaplastic astrocytoma.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Highly likely based on spectroscopy profile
Less likely; would expect less prominent lipid peak and higher NAA

recommended_actions: Tissue biopsy for definitive histological and molecular diagnosis
Discuss surgical planning with neuro-oncology multidisciplinary team

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Negative — no oligoclonal bands detected in CSF or serum
special_tests.MOG_IgG: Negative — myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibodies not detected
special_tests.cytology: Atypical cells present — rare large cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and irregular nuclear contours, suspicious for malignancy. Flow cytometry recommended.
special_tests.flow_cytometry: No evidence of lymphoproliferative disorder; atypical cells are non-lymphoid

output.interpretation: CSF shows mildly elevated protein consistent with disruption of the blood-spinal cord barrier, likely due to the extensive intramedullary lesion. Normal glucose ratio argues against infectious meningitis. The absence of oligoclonal bands and negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies make neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder and multiple sclerosis unlikely. Notably, cytology reveals rare atypical cells suspicious for malignancy, which is a critical finding supporting neoplastic etiology. Normal cell counts without pleocytosis argue against an active inflammatory or infectious process.

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord lesion, enhancing component at C3-C5

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV ratio 3.2 compared to normal-appearing spinal cord, >1.75 threshold for high-grade neoplasm)
signal_characteristics.perfusion_curve: Type III curve with signal intensity remaining below baseline, consistent with high-grade neoplasm

additional_observations: Elevated rCBV in the enhancing component strongly suggests high-grade neoplasm with neoangiogenesis
The perfusion characteristics help distinguish high-grade tumor from lower-grade glioma and demyelination
Non-enhancing portions show lower but still above-normal rCBV, suggesting infiltrative tumor extending beyond enhancement
output.impression: MRI perfusion demonstrates elevated rCBV in the enhancing component of the cervical cord lesion, consistent with a hypervascular high-grade neoplasm. This supports the diagnosis of glioblastoma or anaplastic astrocytoma over lower-grade glioma or demyelinating disease.
recommended_actions: Surgical biopsy for definitive tissue diagnosis

Histopathology.clinical_significance: Hypercellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia
High proliferative index consistent with high-grade malignancy

Molecular Markers.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; associated with poorer prognosis compared to IDH-mutant tumors
Unmethylated MGMT promoter associated with reduced benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy
High proliferative index consistent with aggressive tumor biology
EGFR amplification common in IDH-wildtype GBM; potential therapeutic target
TERT promoter mutation supports IDH-wildtype GBM molecular classification

output.interpretation: Biopsy confirms glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV. Key molecular features include unmethylated MGMT promoter (reduced temozolomide benefit), EGFR amplification, and TERT promoter mutation. No 1p/19q codeletion (not oligodendroglioma). This is a primary (de novo) glioblastoma arising in the cervical spinal cord, a rare but documented location. The NF1 background may be associated with increased glioma risk. Neuro-oncology multidisciplinary team discussion recommended for treatment planning.
abnormal_values_summary: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV confirmed

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord C2-C7 extending to lower medulla (as previously described)
findings.mass_effect: Marked cord expansion with CSF effacement

signal_characteristics.T2: Mild hyperintensity within the cord parenchyma

additional_observations: No evidence of leptomeningeal enhancement along the thoracic or lumbar spine
Conus medullaris and cauda equina are normal in appearance
No additional intramedullary lesions identified in the thoracic or lumbar cord
Mild T2 signal changes at T1-T3 likely represent perilesional edema rather than tumor extension
output.impression: Complete spinal axis MRI demonstrates the known extensive cervical cord lesion without evidence of drop metastases or leptomeningeal dissemination. Mild perilesional edema extends to T1-T3 without enhancement, suggesting reactive changes rather than tumor extension at this level.
recommended_actions: Baseline complete spinal MRI established for future comparison
Periodic surveillance imaging recommended to monitor for leptomeningeal spread

output.query: neurofibromatosis type 1 spinal cord glioblastoma intramedullary

results.title: Spinal cord glioblastoma in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1: a case report and review
results.summary: Reports a case of spinal cord GBM in a 34-year-old woman with NF1. NF1 patients have an increased risk of CNS tumors, most commonly pilocytic astrocytomas and optic pathway gliomas. Spinal cord GBM is rare even in NF1 patients. The case demonstrates that high-grade gliomas should be considered in the differential when NF1 patients present with spinal cord lesions showing atypical imaging features.

results.title: Malignant transformation of spinal cord tumors in neurofibromatosis type 1: systematic review
results.summary: Systematic review of 42 cases of malignant spinal cord tumors in NF1 patients. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNSTs) are the most common malignant tumor, but high-grade astrocytomas including GBM have been reported. Risk factors for malignant transformation include prior radiation therapy and older age at presentation.

results.title: Intramedullary spinal cord tumors in adults: a review of clinical features, diagnosis, and treatment
results.summary: Intramedullary spinal cord tumors represent 2-4% of all CNS tumors. Ependymomas and astrocytomas are most common. GBM of the spinal cord is rare, comprising <1.5% of all spinal cord tumors. Prognosis is poor with median survival of 11-15 months. Standard treatment involves maximal safe resection followed by radiation therapy, with or without temozolomide.

output.summary: Literature search reveals that spinal cord glioblastoma is rare, comprising less than 1.5% of spinal cord tumors. In NF1 patients, pilocytic astrocytoma is far more common, but high-grade gliomas including GBM do occur and should be considered when imaging features are atypical for pilocytic astrocytoma. Key distinguishing features include heterogeneous enhancement with areas of necrosis, elevated rCBV on perfusion, and aggressive metabolic profile on spectroscopy. Prognosis for spinal cord GBM is poor (median survival 11-15 months). Treatment typically involves maximal safe resection followed by radiation, with temozolomide having limited benefit in MGMT-unmethylated tumors.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during concurrent radiation therapy
interactions: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel: Temozolomide may reduce efficacy of hormonal contraceptives through hepatic enzyme induction; recommend alternative or additional barrier contraception during treatment
Sumatriptan: No significant interaction expected, but both can cause nausea; monitor for additive GI side effects
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to temozolomide or dacarbazine
Severe myelosuppression — must monitor CBC weekly during concurrent chemoradiation
warnings: Myelosuppression is the dose-limiting toxicity; monitor CBC with differential weekly during concurrent phase and before each 28-day cycle
Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is required during concurrent chemoradiation phase
Hepatotoxicity possible; monitor liver function tests periodically
Teratogenic — pregnancy must be avoided during treatment and for 6 months after; current oral contraceptive may have reduced efficacy
Nausea and vomiting common; prescribe prophylactic antiemetic (ondansetron)
MGMT promoter unmethylated status in this case suggests reduced clinical benefit from temozolomide
output.formulary_status: On formulary; requires neuro-oncology attending authorization for concurrent chemoradiation protocol
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) — alternative alkylating agent for patients intolerant to temozolomide
Bevacizumab — anti-VEGF therapy for recurrent GBM
Tumor treating fields (TTFields) — adjunctive therapy for supratentorial GBM (limited data for spinal cord location)
Clinical trial enrollment should be strongly considered given the rare tumor location and unfavorable molecular markers

output.query: differentiating NMOSD from intramedullary spinal cord tumor area postrema MRI

results.title: Area postrema lesions in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder: MRI features and clinical significance
results.summary: Area postrema involvement is a hallmark of NMOSD and is included in the 2015 diagnostic criteria. However, tumors extending from the cervical cord to the medulla can also involve this region. Key differentiating features: NMOSD lesions are typically non-enhancing or show faint enhancement, resolve with immunotherapy, and are associated with AQP4-IgG positivity. Tumors show heterogeneous enhancement, mass effect, and progressive course.

results.title: 2015 NMOSD diagnostic criteria: practical application and pitfalls
results.summary: NMOSD diagnosis requires either AQP4-IgG positivity with at least one core clinical characteristic, or AQP4-IgG negativity with at least two core characteristics and spatial dissemination. A single spinal cord attack without AQP4-IgG positivity does not meet diagnostic criteria. The criteria emphasize the importance of excluding alternative diagnoses.

output.summary: The involvement of the area postrema in the lower medulla initially raises consideration of NMOSD, which classically involves this region. However, several features distinguish tumor from NMOSD in this case: (1) heterogeneous enhancement pattern with areas of necrosis favors tumor, (2) negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies, (3) absence of oligoclonal bands, (4) progressive rather than relapsing course, (5) mass effect and cord expansion beyond what is typical for NMOSD, and (6) the 2015 NMOSD criteria require either AQP4 positivity or at least two core clinical characteristics with spatial dissemination, neither of which is met here.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV, of the cervical spinal cord

differential.key_distinguishing: Most common intramedullary tumor in NF1 patients, but imaging here lacks the classic cystic mass with enhancing mural nodule pattern and shows atypical features including heterogeneous enhancement and areas suggesting necrosis
Second most common intramedullary tumor in adults and reported in NF1; typically central in location with cap sign (hemosiderin) on MRI and more well-defined borders

Area postrema involvement and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis pattern are features of NMOSD, but negative AQP4 antibodies, heterogeneous enhancement with necrosis, and mass effect argue strongly against this diagnosis
NF1 patients have elevated risk of MPNST; however, these typically arise from peripheral nerves or nerve roots rather than presenting as intramedullary lesions
Can present similarly to GBM but typically lacks the palisading necrosis and microvascular proliferation that define GBM; tissue biopsy is required for definitive distinction

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain and cervical spine MRI with contrast to characterize the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Extensive infiltrative intramedullary cervical cord lesion with heterogeneous enhancement, exophytic component, and extension to area postrema

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, liver function, coagulation panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal baseline labs suitable for surgical and chemotherapy planning

optimal_actions.action: Perform EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity and baseline cerebral function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Nonspecific generalized slowing without focal cortical epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Request CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands, AQP4 antibodies, MOG antibodies, and cytology to differentiate neoplasm from demyelinating disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated protein, negative OCBs, negative AQP4/MOG antibodies, atypical cells suspicious for malignancy on cytology

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy of the lesion to characterize metabolite profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid-lactate peak consistent with high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Request MRI perfusion to assess tumor vascularity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV consistent with high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Request complete spinal axis MRI to evaluate for drop metastases
Arrange tissue biopsy for histological and molecular characterization (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)
Check drug interactions for temozolomide with current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Interaction with oral contraceptives noted; myelosuppression monitoring required

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature for NF1-associated spinal cord glioblastoma management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Rare entity with poor prognosis; standard treatment is maximal safe resection plus radiation with or without temozolomide

critical_actions: Obtain MRI of the cervical spine and brain with contrast to characterize the lesion and rule out intracranial pathology
Test for AQP4 antibodies and oligoclonal bands to exclude NMOSD and MS before proceeding with biopsy
Obtain tissue diagnosis via biopsy with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) before initiating definitive treatment
Perform complete spinal axis MRI to assess for leptomeningeal dissemination
Refer to neuro-oncology multidisciplinary team for treatment planning
Initiate dexamethasone for spinal cord edema and mass effect to prevent further neurological deterioration
contraindicated_actions: Initiating high-dose corticosteroids without first obtaining diagnostic imaging and antibody testing, as steroids could confound the diagnostic workup for demyelinating disease
Assuming the diagnosis is pilocytic astrocytoma based solely on the NF1 history without obtaining tissue confirmation
Performing lumbar puncture without first confirming there is no obstructive hydrocephalus or significant mass effect at the craniocervical junction
Delaying biopsy in favor of empiric immunosuppressive therapy for presumed NMOSD without supporting serological evidence
Starting temozolomide without tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling
Withholding anticoagulation prophylaxis in an immobilized patient without documented contraindication
key_reasoning_points: In NF1 patients, pilocytic astrocytoma is the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumor, but the imaging features here — heterogeneous enhancement, areas of necrosis, and infiltrative margins — are atypical for pilocytic astrocytoma and should raise suspicion for a higher-grade neoplasm

The lesion's extension to the area postrema initially mimics NMOSD, which classically involves this region, but the negative AQP4 antibodies, enhancement pattern with necrosis, and mass effect argue strongly against demyelinating disease
Ependymoma is also reported in NF1 and should be in the differential, but ependymomas are typically more central, well-demarcated, and show a hemosiderin cap sign

The 2015 NMOSD diagnostic criteria require either AQP4-IgG positivity with a core characteristic, or two core characteristics with spatial dissemination in the AQP4-seronegative setting — neither criterion is met here, effectively excluding NMOSD
CSF cytology showing atypical cells suspicious for malignancy is a critical finding that shifts the differential decisively toward neoplasm
The mildly elevated TSH is a red herring — it represents subclinical hypothyroidism that does not explain the acute myelopathic presentation and should not distract from the spinal cord lesion workup
The exophytic component of the lesion, while unusual for a typical GBM, has been described in spinal cord gliomas and should not lead the clinician to incorrectly favor a nerve sheath tumor diagnosis
Molecular profiling is essential — the IDH-wildtype and MGMT-unmethylated status have direct implications for treatment planning and prognosis

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: The NF1 background creates a strong prior toward pilocytic astrocytoma, and the area postrema involvement introduces NMOSD as a plausible differential. The mildly elevated TSH adds a minor red herring. The imaging features are atypical for the expected NF1-associated tumor, requiring the agent to synthesize imaging characteristics, serology, and CSF findings to reach the correct diagnosis of high-grade glioma.
